All right, so we just crushed the classic upsell, the menu upsell, the anchor upsell. Now let's talk about the rollover upsell. Rollover upsell of these four upsells is probably my most used upsell. Not necessarily my favorite, but my most commonly used. So let's break down how this came to be. So I learned this from when I was doing the when-your-money back as the attraction offer. So everyone kept crediting their money towards the membership, but I would put it all upfront, right? The membership was for me $150 a month. So $600 was four months of free service. So they did six weeks, and then they would essentially get another four months for free, and it would almost take me a half year to get my first payment from people coming in the door, which sucked, right? So the thing is, is for them, they didn't feel the payment, so they stopped coming, and so I needed a way to upsell people into something paid. And so I drove to a friend of mine, actually here in Vegas now, to learn more about how he ran his gym. And the thing is I studied everything he was doing, because he was just doing way better than I was. And he said in his off-handed comment that he was getting 70% plus people stay on afterwards, and pay, and I was like, how are you doing that? He's like, oh yeah, he's like, I just take their winnings and credit towards a year. And he said it just like that. And I was like, that's it. All you do is you take whatever they just paid for, and you roll it. And so that's the upsell. It's whatever you paid for, I'll just credit it towards the next thing. So let's get into the description, here's how it works. So roll over upsells, credit some or all of a customer's previous purchase towards your next offer. And this, in my experience, gets way more people to take it. So once I know how much credit to give, I figure out three things. One, who to upsell, two, what to upsell, and three, how to roll over the credit. So for the who, I use roll over upsells in four situations. First is to re-engage customers who left a while ago. I call this a win-back campaign. You reach out to old customers and say, hey, Sandra, you haven't been back in six months, but I saw that I owed you some money. And so the subject would be I owe you money, and people tend to respond to that. And so you say, hey, I owe you 500 bucks. And she's like, what do you mean? You owe me $500. I was like, well, I promised you this thing, and you haven't come back in a while. So I want to take $500. Now that could be 100% of what they paid. It could be a third of what they paid so far, whatever. And say, and I want to get you in here, and I'll credit towards this other service thing. And so a lot of people will take that offer to credit whatever they've paid up to this point for more help. Great, and that's how you can re-engage old people who haven't been back in a while. The second is to rescue upset customers as a better alternative to a refund. So someone says, hey, I hate you, and you say, hey, no big deal, this $500 that you paid, I'll just credit towards the thing I should have sold you originally. So you got all the stuff that you just got for free. It's on me. Let's just give you the thing you really need. So I'd rather do a redo than a refund. And that's how we use the roll over there. The third is to rescue other people's upset customers. This is particularly savage. So what you do is you go down the one star reviews of all your competitors, and you message those people, and you say, hey, how much money did you spend at So-and-So competitor? And they're like $1,200. Fuck that guy. And you're like, you know what? I'll give you the $1,200 of credit towards my stuff, because I just want you to get this problem solved. You turn their biggest hater into your biggest ambassador. Savage. Number four, you upsell your regular customers walking the door. So there's the four hoos. The next one is the what. So remember, you can upsell more of what they just got, something better or something new and or different. So to make money, roll their credit over to something more expensive. And so the more expensive thing, again, in my opinion, should be five times more expensive. So if I'm going to roll a $500 thing, I'm going to roll it towards a $2,025 plus type offer. For the how, you can apply all or part of the discount upfront, or you can spread it over time. And so this is dependent on you. So in the examples I gave before, I took it and I spread it over time. You could, if you had a smaller amount, do it upfront. It's up to you. You can also make up a fee that you can then wave and use the credit for. There's lots of things you can do here. All right, but fundamentally, you can just figure out now, the all or part of it is like, let's say somebody was with you for three years and they spent five grand with you, you might not credit five grand towards something else. If your services, you know, aren't that much, maybe it's $2,000 a year, $5,000, three years of service. Well, you'd certainly want to get that customer back. But if you give them another three years, it doesn't make sense. So you can say, hey, I'm gonna give you the last, you know, the last three things you bought for me. Let me credit those instead. Right, you wouldn't say instead, you'd say, I'll credit those for you. Right, all of those things, you can say, listen, I'm gonna assume that we did something to mess up because you didn't come back. And so I'm gonna assume that the last six months of service that we did was not up to snuff. So let me make it up to you. So examples. So chiropractor, re-engage old patients with the Win Back campaign. So you say, who's the who? Six months since their last purchase. What? New plan, how? Upfront. So that's our who, our what and our how. So reach out to your old patients, you look at their purchase history, you offer to apply, some are all their past purchase towards something more expensive than what they bought. So I would say, hey, Mrs. Banks, I wanted to give your money back. Do you have a minute? Great, yeah, I wanted to see how your back pain's gone. Oh, I'm so sorry to hear that. Well, I have got some good news. As a way of saying thank you, I wanna give you $500 your money back is credit towards staying pain free for good. Is that of any interest? Great, let's get you in. Let's say a dentist, hey, save your own upset customer with a roll over upsell. So who upset customer? What, teeth whitening, how? Frontload $200 credit. So we say, hey, the person pays $200 for teeth cleaning, but doesn't think their teeth got whiter. We explain they need to get more and upsell into a teeth whitening package, which includes multiple sessions and at home kit and multiple deep cleanings. Then you offer to credit the $200, they just paid for the cleaning towards the whitening package. This just really works with people who are like, well, this isn't what I thought it was gonna be. And you're like, oh, that's on me. I thought you wanted this, miscommunication. I'll make it up to you. Let me take everything you paid so you didn't lose anything. Everything I was for free now. And let's put it towards the thing you ultimately wanted. The thing is, is they still want the thing they spent money on. And so if what they really wanted was higher expectations, then sell them the higher package. Rescue, cough, steal, other people's upset customers. So who competitors customers? What, service agreement? How, roll over costs to break old agreement? So let's say you find a competitor's upset customers and credit their old purchase with them towards a new purchase with you. Then you roll over the amount, they owe them as a credit towards a longer agreement with you. So let's say they're like, ah, you know, I would, but I've got three months left in this service agreement. You say, no worries. Whatever you have the three months, I'll credit it towards mine and I'll just make mine a two year agreement. So you'd say like, hey, John, saw your negative review on their product and it really upset me to make it up to you. I'll credit whatever payments you have left with them to switch to ours. This way you don't lose a thing and you start getting the benefits now, fair enough. Because that way you don't have to wait for that thing to expire and pay and then wait to get the benefits. You can start getting the benefits now and you don't lose anything. And I get the customer membership. So this is where you spread the first purchase over time. So who current customers? What 12 month membership? How spread over the first purchase? So if somebody comes in, buys a block of service or of membership time, as soon as they do, you can then offer to apply the entire amount towards more time, like 12 months. I can do rollover upsells anytime. I just prefer to do them right then. All right, so this is the important points. Use rollover offers to attract new customers. For example, you rollover summer all over the customers paid somebody else towards your thing and you can find leads by scraping contact information from negative product reviews wherever available. You can do rollover upsells before refunding. This has solved me tons of cost, saved me tons of customers in cash. If you did a bad job, hey, it happens rollover for a do over. And if they want something different, you can rollover the purchase toward that thing instead. Previous customer are still customers, upsell them. These are win backs. So reach out to old customers, six plus months from their last purchase, look at how much they paid, decide how much you want to give them, offer it. Example, I made personalized videos. This is me personally. I made personalized videos for 200 past customers. This is before AI and all that stuff. It took me like two days. Offering them $4,000 a credit to return. Add urgency to rollover upsells. So make them one time only. So this is a key point. So this is optional, it's up to you, but this is how I prefer to do it. Make the moment you present the offer the only time to take the offer. So they don't get to sleep on it. So you want to surprise and delight them with this credit or this discount. So if they want the credit, they got to take it now. If not, no big deal. They can always pay full price later. How to price the rollover upsell. So to make money on a discounted offer, you have to have profit leftover in order to discount it, right? So I try to make the upsell at least four times more than the rollover credit. So that gives me a 25% discount at most if it's four times more expensive. Remember, the rules of discounting apply. Bigger discounts to make you less profit per sale, but they get you more sales. Now, you don't need to credit the entire amount. I just like to highlight this again because people do this and then they ruin their businesses. So I'm telling you, don't do that. Like you can just take as much or as little as you want. The goal is to simply get as much of a discount that you get responses. So if you find out that $200 is enough to move the needle and the average person spent $2,000 a year, then you can say, hey, your last purchase with me of the 2000 you spent last year was this thing. And that's what you choose to credit. You don't have to credit everything. If the person spent a very small time with you and you have really big packages, credit the whole thing. If they spent a really long time with you and you have small tickets, credit the last one. So I rollover whatever I think would incentivize to buy the next thing. And you can just test to find the sweets about it. From the beginning, it's $500, you could test 300, whatever. This is my famous gift card play. The way that this works, and this is a savage play, and it basically is a rollover, just like packaged differently, is that you say, hey, this works great for like holiday seasons, things like that. You say, hey, this also crushes for Black Friday. I'm giving $200 gift cards away for 90% off. Limit two per person, and you can only use them on someone else because it's the holiday season. So you can do this for Christmas and you can do this for Black Friday. You can use it twice if you want. Now, the key here, you can also do it for birthdays. Sorry, not birthdays, sorry. They give it to other people as birthday gifts. Anyways, when customers buy the gift card, ask them who they want to make it out to and if they'll make an introduction. Then when that person comes in, the person they introduce comes in, you roll their gift card over into the main offer. And so some people see this and are like, you want me to give 90% off my main stuff? It's like, no, bro, I want you to sell a $2,000 thing and give $200 gift cards away for 20 bucks. So what's really happening here? You're gonna get paid $20 to get a lead that's a hot referral that you're then gonna take the $200 and roll it into a $2,000 product, which you may have marked up by $200. Or you add something to it, that's high margin, that you can justify adding the $200 for that then you can take the deduction off of. All right, so you make the value of the gift card 20% of the price or whatever you wanna sell next. So if it's $200 gift card, you make that one fifth of the price, $200. So if I had a $2,000 off, that'd be one tenth. But minimum should be five X would be a $1,000 offer. Obviously you can make it more expensive than that. You just want it to be enough that it drives a purchase. All right, in our example, we sell $200 gift card for 20 bucks, then apply that $200 offer with at least a $1,000 price tag. People pay you to refer their friends, plus you get cash from unused gift cards. Wins all around, that is the gift card play. Final points, roll over upsells, credit summer, all of the customers pre-purchases towards your next offer. Do roll over upsells, figure out who to upsell, what to upsell, and how to credit the roll over. Who to upsell, old customers, upset customers, other people's upset customers, current customers. What to upsell, more of something, better of something, new something new, just make sure that you make a profit after applying your discount. How to roll over the credit, you can do full or partial purchase, give it upfront or you can spread it out. We went through examples of all those. You can price your next offer for at least four times higher than the credit, so this makes it a 25% discount. Everything above that is gravy, I wouldn't do less than that. And to get more takers, add urgency, make your roll over credit a one time only offer. And boom, that is the roll over upsell.
